blurb: Also called perennial statice — a tough, drought- and salt-proof steppe perennial that throws a huge airy 'baby's-breath' cloud of tiny papery lavender-blue flowers in midsummer over a basal rosette of broad leathery leaves; one of the best fresh-or-dried everlasting cut flowers for the dry Front Range.
water: Low; xeric once established, tolerates drought and dry soil
spread: Clumping; a single non-running rosette from a deep taproot, may self-seed
seasons: Basal rosette of dark spoon-shaped leaves spring through fall; airy lavender-blue flower cloud July–Aug; the branched flower stems dry to a buff 'everlasting' skeleton that persists into winter
wildlife: Bees, butterflies and other pollinators work the tiny flowers; the airy bloom is a favorite of small native bees
toxic: No toxicity reported; grown as an ornamental and cut/dried flower, not a food plant
origin: Steppes of SE & central Europe to the Caucasus (Black Sea region)
habitat: Dry saline steppe and rocky calcareous slopes

care.hardiness: Very cold-hardy — rated USDA zones 3–9, so it is bulletproof across the Front Range (zones 5b–6a) and shrugs off Boulder's winter lows.[1][3] A steppe plant from the same dry-continental climate as the high plains, it is one of the best-adapted perennials for our hot, dry summers and cold winters; Denver Botanic Gardens grows it in its Steppe collection as a Zone-3 xeric perennial.[3] The one non-negotiable is sharp winter drainage — wet, heavy soil in winter causes crown rot.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring (Apr–May) from a young nursery pot, while the deep taproot is still small enough to settle in. Set the crown at soil level in a sunny, sharply drained spot and water it through the first season to establish; after that it largely fends for itself. It resents being moved, so site it permanently the first time. Sea lavender is a poor container subject long-term — the deep taproot wants open ground — but a young plant can be held a season in a deep, gritty, free-draining pot before going in the ground.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — 6+ hours of direct sun for the strongest bloom cloud and a sturdy, self-supporting plant; it will flop and bloom thinly in shade.[1][2]
care.soil: Average, medium, well-drained soil; it does best in light, sandy, well-drained loam and tolerates lean, shallow-rocky ground.[1] Drainage matters far more than fertility — overly rich soil grows lush leaves at the expense of the flower cloud, and waterlogged or heavy clay invites crown rot, so amend tight Boulder clay with grit/coarse sand. Native to saline steppe, it is notably salt-tolerant, useful near walks and drives that get winter de-icing.[1][2]
care.water: Low. Water regularly the first season to establish the deep taproot; after that it is genuinely xeric and tolerates drought and dry soil with little or no supplemental water.[1] Avoid keeping the crown wet — especially over winter — which is the main thing that kills it.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants 18–24 in apart — this open spacing gives each rosette room and, just as importantly, the good air circulation that prevents crown and root rot.[1]
care.propagation: Best from seed or root cuttings; division is difficult and resented because of the long taproot, so do not dig up and split an established clump.[1][2] Sow seed in spring on the soil surface (it needs light to germinate), keeping it evenly moist but not wet — germination runs roughly two to four weeks at about 68–77°F.[1][2] Root cuttings taken from the thick taproot are the reliable vegetative route. Seed-grown plants take a few years to reach full flowering size, and established plants may also self-seed gently.[1]
care.bloom: The signature airy, cloud-like mass of tiny lavender-blue flowers opens in midsummer — roughly July into August on the Front Range — held on long, wiry, much-branched stems above the foliage.[1][3] The papery flowers (technically long-lasting colored calyces) hold their color for weeks and are superb cut, fresh or dried.[1]
care.maintenance: Very low-maintenance.[1] Leave the dried flower stems standing for winter structure, or cut them for everlasting arrangements; tidy the spent stems back to the basal rosette in late winter or early spring before new growth pushes. Tall plants in heavy, rich soil can flop and may need a light stake, but in lean, sunny ground they stand on their own.[1] No routine feeding is needed.
care.troubles: Few problems in a sunny, well-drained site.[1] Crown and root rot are the main risk — almost always a symptom of poor drainage or a wet winter crown — and botrytis and other fungal diseases can appear in damp, crowded conditions; good air circulation and sharp drainage are the whole prescription.[1][2] Otherwise it is largely pest- and disease-free and deer tend to leave it alone.
care.companions: Pairs naturally with other sun-loving, drought-tolerant steppe and prairie plants on lean, well-drained ground — ornamental grasses, yarrow, Russian sage, lavender, catmint, and globe thistle — where its lavender-blue haze softens bolder neighbors. Use it the way you'd use baby's breath, as an airy filler in a xeric border or cutting bed.[3]
care.harvest: For drying, cut the flower stems just as most of the tiny flowers in the cloud have opened and show full color, in the cool of the morning.[1] Strip the lower foliage, bundle loosely, and hang the bunches upside down in a dark, dry, airy place; the papery calyces hold their lavender-blue color for months, making sea lavender a premier everlasting for winter arrangements.[1]

edible.summary: Sea lavender is grown as an ornamental and cut/dried flower — there is no documented edible part, so don't eat it; it is not notably poisonous, just not a food.[1][2]
edible.caution: Limonium platyphyllum is cultivated strictly as an ornamental and an everlasting cut flower. Authoritative horticultural sources record no culinary use and no toxicity rating — it is neither a recognized edible nor a known poison.[1][2] With no positive food record from a reliable source, treat it as a non-food plant and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots. (Note the look-alike trap: the dried flower clouds sold as florist 'statice' come from this genus and its relatives and are decorative material, not food.)
